findings.description: The entire roof surface within the bounding box is a bright white/light gray color — starkly different from every surrounding home's dark gray asphalt shingle roofs. This is consistent with a flat or very low-slope membrane roofing system (e.g., TPO or modified bitumen) rather than traditional pitched shingles.
Multiple small dark circular/dot-shaped objects are scattered across the light roof surface at irregular intervals — at least 6–8 visible items distributed across the field of the roof.
findings.location_description: Scattered across the central and south portions of the flat roof

findings.description: Large quantities of stacked lumber and construction materials are visibly piled along the east and south perimeter of the subject property, directly adjacent to the structure.
findings.location_description: East and south perimeter of subject property, at roof/ground level

rationale: The subject roof within the orange bounding box is unmistakably different from all neighboring homes, which uniformly feature dark gray pitched asphalt shingle roofs. The subject roof presents a very bright white/light gray flat or very low-slope surface — characteristic of a membrane roofing system (TPO, EPDM, or modified bitumen). Multiple small dark objects are scattered across the roof at irregular intervals, which are visible at this resolution and warrant a closer look. Most notably, large piles of lumber and construction materials are stacked around the building's perimeter, strongly indicating this property is either under active construction or undergoing a major renovation/re-roof. Because the roofing system type and completion status cannot be fully determined from aerial imagery, and given the active construction context, this roof is rated 'concerning' and an in-person inspection is essential to confirm roofing material, installation stage, and any existing condition issues.
